narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds, estimated a 74.5% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger projected outcomes to maintain high bar passage rates. For context, the model looks at patterns from thousands of past students, including their early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPAs, family income levels, and whether they plan to study full-time or part-time.

The three biggest factors pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your part-time enrollment status. Your Year 3 decile of 1—meaning you ranked in the bottom tenth of your school that year—had the strongest negative impact, as it signaled to the model a significant drop-off in performance compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1 reinforced this concern, being well below the dataset average of about 5.7, suggesting early struggles that often predict ongoing challenges in rigorous legal training.

Your choice of part-time study, which is uncommon (only about 7% of students opt for it versus over 90% full-time), further tipped the scales toward failure in the model's eyes, likely because part-time schedules have historically correlated with lower bar passage rates in the data. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 and LSAT score of 31 are also below averages (3.21 and 36.7, respectively), and your middle family income bracket is typical, they played smaller roles here compared to those top three influences. As a Black male applicant, your profile reflects ambitions in a field where systemic barriers can make outcomes tougher, but the model focuses purely on these measurable predictors without bias adjustments.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your high school deciles through additional coursework or community college classes if possible, and exploring full-time program options that align better with proven success paths. Building a stronger narrative around overcoming early academic hurdles—perhaps through targeted test prep or GPA-improvement strategies—could help in reapplying to schools with more flexible criteria. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so persistence with focused improvements will make a real difference next time.
